mnemonics: CONNECTed spaces have no DISCONNECTion: A space is connected if it cannot be split into two non-empty, disjoint open sets.
Path-Connected is Stronger: If you can draw a path (a continuous curve) between any two points, the space is connected. But not all connected spaces allow this (e.g., the Topologist's Sine Curve).
keyPoints: A metric space is connected if it is not the union of two non-empty, disjoint open sets.
Intervals in R are the canonical examples of connected sets.
The continuous image of a connected set is connected. This is a crucial property for theorems like the Intermediate Value Theorem.
Path-connectedness implies connectedness, but the converse is not always true.
commonMistakes: Assuming a set is disconnected because it looks 'separated'. One must check for the existence of two open sets in the parent space that separate it.
Confusing 'closed' with 'disconnected'. A set can be closed and connected (e.g., a closed interval) or closed and disconnected (e.g., two separate points).
Believing path-connectedness and connectedness are equivalent. Path-connectedness is a stronger condition.
